title: Mud on Carpet
stainChemistry: Same chemistry as mud on fabric — clay + silt + sand + organic debris — but on carpet the failure mode is different. Rubbing wet mud on a carpet pile both (a) drives particles deeper into the backing and (b) can damage the face yarn by grinding abrasive soil against the tufts. Let it dry, vacuum it, done. Most mud on carpet doesn't need any chemistry at all. Nylon / olefin / polyester / wool all tolerate dry vacuuming equally well. Wet chemistry follows the same cold-damp-cloth + weighted extraction pattern as other carpet spills if any shadow remains.
whyThisWorks: Dry + vacuum is 90% of the protocol. What's left after a thorough vacuum is fine-particle residue and maybe an earth-tone shadow if the mud had clay or organic matter. NSD via damp cloth handles both. Resist any instinct to pour water on wet mud on carpet — you drive the particles into the pad where they don't come out.
defaultAssumption: Most home failures on mud-carpet come from panic-scrubbing wet mud. The dry-first rule is counterintuitive but it's the whole protocol. Synthetic carpet assumption.

spottingProtocol.agent: Let It Dry, Do Not Touch
spottingProtocol.instruction: Leave the wet mud alone. Don't step on it, don't try to blot it, don't dab cleaner on it. Let it dry completely. If the spot is large and wet, set a fan nearby to accelerate drying. Do NOT apply heat (hair dryer, space heater) — slow, natural air drying preserves the mud's dry-brittle structure which is what lifts cleanly.

spottingProtocol.technique: Multiple passes with a good vacuum
spottingProtocol.equipment: Upright or canister vacuum with beater bar
spottingProtocol.instruction: Vacuum the dried mud. Use the beater bar to lift dried particles from the pile. Multiple passes in different directions. Most of the mud leaves in this step.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD in warm water, damp cloth, weighted dry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: For any residual shadow: apply NSD via DAMP cloth — press onto the spot, lift, rotate. Never pour. Extract between passes with a weighted dry towel. 2–3 cycles usually clears residual soil.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dilute acetic acid 5% OR lemon juice 1:4 with water, damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: For red-clay mud with iron content leaving a rust-like tint: dab dilute acid via damp cloth. Blot with dry towel.
Fan-dry the spot. Once bone-dry, vacuum once more to lift any last particles the wet chemistry may have loosened.

steps: 1. Let mud dry completely in place. Fan if large. No heat.
2. Vacuum thoroughly — beater bar, multiple directions.
3. NSD via damp cloth + weighted extraction for residual shadow.
4. Acid rinse for red-clay iron tint if present.
warnings: Never rub or wet-scrub fresh mud — grinds into pad
Never apply heat to dry mud — crystallizes it harder against fiber
Never pour water — carpet pad contamination
Red clay iron content = separate acid step

homeSolutions.instruction: Panicking and scrubbing wet mud is the #1 home failure on this stain. DON'T. Step away. Let the mud dry completely on the carpet — 1 to 4 hours depending on how much there is. Wet mud grinds into the pile and soaks into the pad; dry mud lifts with a vacuum. Patience is the whole protocol.

Once the mud is completely dry (it'll be a dusty crust), run a vacuum over the spot. Use the beater bar / rotating brush. Go over it multiple times in different directions. Most of the mud lifts right out. You'll be surprised.

For any shadow left after vacuuming: dampen a white cloth with cool water + a drop of Dawn dish soap. Press it onto the spot, lift, rotate. DO NOT pour water on the carpet. Keep the cloth damp, not wet. 2–3 cycles usually clears the residue.
Between each wet pass, press a thick DRY towel on the spot with a heavy book or pan on top for 15 minutes. This pulls moisture UP out of the carpet backing before it reaches the pad.

If the mud was red clay (common in the Southeast), a faint rust-colored tint may remain from the iron in the soil. Dab a few drops of lemon juice or diluted white vinegar on it via a damp cloth for 2–3 minutes, then blot dry. The mild acid converts the iron to a soluble form.
Point a fan at the spot. Let it dry completely — several hours. Once bone-dry, vacuum once more to lift any particles the wet chemistry loosened.

Mud rarely needs a pro. If a large volume of mud was walked on for days before drying (pad contamination), or two home rounds didn't clear a shadow, call a carpet cleaner. Tell them: "Mud on [nylon / wool / synthetic] carpet, [fresh / dried / walked-on-extensively], home-treated with dry + vacuum + Dawn damp-cloth + [lemon juice for red clay]."

steps: 1. LET IT DRY — 1–4 hours, no scrubbing.
2. Vacuum thoroughly, multiple passes, beater bar.
3. Dawn + cool water on a DAMP cloth for residual shadow.
4. Weighted dry towel 15 min between wet passes.
5. Red clay: lemon juice / dilute vinegar for iron tint.
7. Escalate rarely — walked-on-for-days is the main case.
products: Lemon juice or white vinegar (red clay only)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if walked-on wet mud has contaminated the pad and two home rounds plateau.
warnings: NEVER scrub or rinse wet mud — drives particles into pad
NEVER apply heat to dry mud on carpet

neverDo: Never scrub wet mud — grinds in + spreads
Never apply heat to dry mud — crystallizes worse
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / olefin / polyester / wool — all tolerate dry vacuum
Red clay iron tint may need acid step

materialWarnings: DRY + VACUUM first — chemistry is step 3
Red clay needs a separate iron step

escalation.when: Walked-on wet mud contaminated the pad, or two home rounds plateau on residual shadow.
escalation.whatToTell: "Mud on [carpet fiber] carpet, [fresh / dried / walked-on-for-days], home-treated with dry + vacuum + Dawn damp-cloth + [lemon juice for red clay]."

contextQuestions: Is the mud fresh or dried?
What kind — garden / construction / red clay?
Was it walked on wet or allowed to dry?
Carpet fiber — nylon, olefin, polyester, wool?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Dry-first is the whole game on carpet mud. The biggest failure mode is panic-scrubbing wet mud, which makes every later step harder.
